title: What is a Feature Map?
concept: A feature map is the 2D grid of numbers you get from sliding one filter across an entire image — each cell records how strongly that filter's pattern was detected at that location, not what color the pixel was.

exercises.prompt: Recap: When a filter slides across an image, what does it produce at each stopping point?

options.text: A single number summarizing how well the filter's pattern matched that patch
A brand new full-color copy of that patch
A random number unrelated to the patch

exercises.explain: Right — one number per stop. Line up every one of those numbers in a grid and you get today's topic: a feature map.

cards.title: A map that doesn't show land
cards.body: Imagine a treasure map, but instead of showing mountains and rivers, every square just shows one number: how likely it is that treasure is buried there. That's basically what a feature map is — a map of 'how much' rather than a map of 'what it looks like.'

cards.title: What is a feature map?
cards.body: A feature map is the 2D grid of numbers produced when you slide one filter across an entire image. Each cell in that grid tells you how strongly that filter's specific pattern (like a vertical edge) was found at that exact location — and where it was found.

cards.title: A pattern-detecting flashlight
cards.body: Picture a flashlight that only lights up brightly when it's pointed at a vertical edge, and stays dim everywhere else. Sweep it across the whole image and write down the brightness at every stop — that list of brightness readings, arranged in a grid, is the feature map.

cards.title: Bright where the edge is
cards.body: Say a 3x3 vertical-edge filter scans a photo of a striped shirt. Over a flat, plain patch it outputs a low number like 4. The moment it slides over a sharp vertical stripe boundary, it outputs a high number like 238. A feature map full of mostly small numbers with a few tall spikes right where the stripes are is exactly what a working edge detector looks like.

cards.title: It's not a smaller photo
cards.body: It's tempting to think a feature map is just a shrunk-down, blurrier version of the original picture. It isn't — it can be brightest in a totally different spot than the image itself, because it's not tracking color or brightness, it's tracking wherever ONE specific pattern shows up.

cards.title: Input Image vs. Feature Map
cards.body: Same photo, two very different grids:

left.text:
Grid of colors
e.g. sky, skin, shirt

right.text:
Grid of match strength
e.g. 'how much vertical edge here'

exercises.prompt: What does a single cell in a feature map actually represent?

options.text: How strongly the filter's pattern was detected at that location
The exact RGB color of the original pixel
The file size of that region of the image

exercises.explain: Exactly — each cell is a match-strength reading for one filter's pattern.

exercises.prompt: True or false: A feature map is produced by sliding ONE filter across the entire image.
exercises.explain: True — one filter, swept across the whole image, produces one feature map.

exercises.sentence: A feature map records how ___ a filter's pattern matched at each location in the image.
exercises.explain: Strongly is right — the feature map's numbers are match-strength readings, not colors.

exercises.prompt: Which of these best describes a feature map produced by an eye-detecting filter?

options.text: A grid that's brightest wherever eye-like patterns appear in the photo, dim elsewhere
A perfect copy of the photo but resized smaller
A list of every pixel's exact RGB value

exercises.explain: Right — bright where the filter's pattern (eyes) shows up, dim where it doesn't.

exercises.prompt: A security camera runs a 'face-shaped blob' filter across a hallway photo. In the resulting feature map, one region lights up with very high values, while the rest of the grid stays near zero. What does that tell you?

options.text: That region of the photo strongly matches the face-shaped pattern the filter is tuned to detect — likely where a face is
That region of the photo is the brightest in raw pixel brightness
The feature map is broken because it should show colors, not match strength
The filter compressed that part of the image into a smaller file

exercises.explain: Exactly! High feature map values mark WHERE a pattern was found and HOW strongly — that's the whole point of a feature map. Next: what happens when a layer looks at more than one input channel at once.
